questions.prompt: Identify the internal components of a laser printer labeled 1 through 12.

image.alt: Internal components and paper path of a laser printer

legends.label: Component 1
legends.hint: Detects whether paper is loaded in the tray or if a paper feed error has occurred.

legends.label: Component 2
legends.hint: The central light-sensitive component that holds the electrostatic image drawn by the laser.

legends.label: Component 3
legends.hint: Applies a uniform negative charge to the surface of the imaging drum during the charging phase.

legends.label: Component 4
legends.hint: The light source responsible for exposing the drum and neutralizing specific charged areas.

legends.label: Component 5
legends.hint: The internal optical mirror system that directs the laser beam across the imaging drum.

legends.label: Component 6
legends.hint: Prepares and moves toner particles from the hopper onto the exposed areas of the imaging drum.

legends.label: Component 7
legends.hint: The replaceable reservoir that holds the fine plastic-based powder used for printing.

legends.label: Component 8
legends.hint: Picks up paper from the tray and feeds it into the printer.

legends.label: Component 9
legends.hint: The removable physical tray where the blank paper stock is held.

legends.label: Component 10
legends.hint: Puts a strong positive charge on the back of the paper to pull toner off the drum.

legends.label: Component 11
legends.hint: Physically removes any residual toner dust left on the drum before the next printing cycle.

legends.label: Component 12
legends.hint: Applies intense heat and pressure to permanently bond the loose toner into the paper fibers.

questions.prompt: Analyze each laser printer malfunction scenario and match it with the correct internal component responsible for the issue. Click on the number for the cause of the issue.

rows.hint: The rubber wheels have become smooth, coated in paper dander, or worn down, losing the friction needed to grip the top sheet.

cells.value: The printer sounds like it is trying to print, but the paper never leaves the tray. Alternatively, the printer reports it is out of paper even though the tray is completely full.

rows.hint: The separation pad’s high-friction surface has worn out, failing to hold back the lower sheets when the pickup roller spins.

cells.value: The printer tries to grab one piece of paper but accidentally pulls in 3 to 5 sheets at the exact same time, causing an immediate paper jam right as the paper leaves the tray.

rows.hint: The rollers are dirty, unevenly worn, or out of sync, releasing one side of the paper slightly faster than the other.

cells.value: The paper feeds out of the tray fine, but the printed text is noticeably crooked on the page, or the paper consistently jams right before it goes under the imaging drum.

rows.hint: The photosensitive surface is physically scratched, damaged by light exposure, or electrically shorted.

cells.value: You get a completely blank white page, a completely jet-black page, or a perfectly straight, sharp line or scratch running all the way down every single page.

rows.hint: If the PCR fails to apply the uniform negative charge (-600V), toner will jump onto every square inch of the drum, drowning the page in black.

cells.value: A completely black page, or a repeating background defect that occurs at a mathematically precise interval down the page.

rows.hint: The roller cannot attract toner because the reservoir is empty, or toner has clumped together unevenly inside the hopper.

cells.value: The image prints out, but the text is incredibly light, or there are clean vertical white streaks running down the page where no ink applied at all.

rows.hint: The transfer roller isn't putting a positive charge on the back of the paper. Without that charge, the toner stays on the drum and refuses to jump onto the paper.

cells.value: The printer goes through all the motions, but the paper comes out completely blank or with an image so faint you can barely see it. However, if you open the printer mid-cycle, you can see the toner sitting perfectly on the drum.

rows.hint: The fuser is not reaching its required melting temperature (approx. 180°C). The loose plastic resin particles are sitting on the paper but were never permanently melted into the fibers.

cells.value: The text looks beautiful and crisp when it exits the printer, but the moment you touch the paper, the toner wipes completely off on your fingers like loose charcoal dust.

rows.hint: The rubber scraper blade is nicked or worn out, failing to physically wipe away residual toner left over from the last rotation of the drum.

cells.value: You print a page with a large bold header at the top. Further down the page (or on the next page), you see a faint, blurry 'ghost' echo of that exact same header.

rows.hint: The mechanical gears or reversing rollers responsible for grabbing the sheet at the exit slot and pulling it backward down the return track are broken or obstructed.

cells.value: The printer works perfectly when printing normal 1-page documents. The second you select 'Print on Both Sides,' the paper jams immediately after passing through the fuser.

rows.hint: The multi-stage transfer belt is slipping or failing to sync with the individual color drums.

cells.value: You print a color photo, but the cyan, magenta, and yellow layers don't line up correctly, making the image look blurry, misaligned, or giving it a 3D-glasses effect.

questions.prompt: Analyze each impact (dot-matrix) printer issue and match it with the component responsible for the failure.

rows.hint: One of the physical metal pins inside the print head is stuck or broken, failing to strike the ribbon.

cells.value: A multi-part invoice prints out, but a consistent horizontal white line runs through every single line of text across the entire page.

rows.hint: The fabric ink ribbon is worn down, dried out, or has become physically unspooled/jammed inside its housing.

cells.value: The printer executes its mechanical tracking correctly, but the text is universally faded, light, or completely invisible.

rows.hint: The tractor feed sprockets are misaligned, or the paper tension bars are clamped down too tightly.

cells.value: Continuous-form paper (the paper with holes on the sides) constantly tears at the seams, slips off track, or jams inside the feed mechanism.

questions.prompt: Analyze each thermal printer failure scenario and match it with the component or issue responsible.

rows.hint: The thermal print head element is burned out, or it isn't receiving power from the internal board.

cells.value: The receipt paper feeds through the machine normally, but the emerging paper is completely blank.

rows.hint: Thermal paper is chemically treated and heat-sensitive; it only has one active side. If it prints blank, the roll was installed upside down. If it turns black, it was exposed to ambient heat.

cells.value: A technician installs a new roll of paper, but the printer outputs completely blank pages. Alternatively, a receipt left in a hot service vehicle turns completely black.

rows.hint: The heating element has collected adhesive residue from sticky labels or paper dander. (A+ Solution: Clean the element with Isopropyl Alcohol (IPA) and a lint-free swab).

cells.value: The text on a barcode label is light, smudged, or missing small segments consistently.

questions.prompt: Analyze each inkjet printer failure scenario and match it with the correct component or maintenance procedure responsible.

rows.hint: A cartridge is empty, or the protective plastic shipping tape was left covering the cartridge’s electrical contacts.

cells.label: Row 1 Component
options: Protective Tape Left on Ink Cartridge

cells.value: Colors are missing from a printed image, or the printer control panel displays an explicit error even though a cartridge was just replaced.

rows.hint: Ink has dried and clogged the microscopic print head nozzles. (A+ Solution: Run an internal head cleaning cycle).

cells.label: Row 2 Component
options: Protective Tape Left on Ink Cartridge

cells.value: Printed documents have consistent horizontal white lines streaking through text and graphics, or specific colors fail to print entirely.

rows.hint: The carriage belt that moves the print head back and forth is slipping, or a paper scrap is blocking the carriage path.

cells.value: The printer makes a sharp grinding sound and the print job halts mid-cycle, or text is printed completely misaligned and garbled horizontally across the page.

rows.hint: The print heads are out of alignment. (A+ Solution: Run the alignment/calibration utility).

cells.value: Lines that should be perfectly straight look jagged, or black text has a blurry, doubled edge.

correct_answers.row1_component: Protective Tape Left on Ink Cartridge

questions.prompt: Identify the correct hard drive form factor based on its spindle speeds and primary deployment scenario.

columns.label: Primary A+ Exam Target / Usage Scenario

rows.hint: This size is the standard for desktop towers, Network Attached Storage (NAS) appliances, and large-scale SAN storage arrays running high-speed mechanical drives.

cells.value: Optimized strictly for raw capacity and legacy high-availability array configurations. This standard footprint is commonly deployed inside stationary system tower enclosures, network-attached storage (NAS) appliances, and full-depth server rack backplanes.

rows.hint: This size is universally used for consumer laptop computers, small-form-factor (SFF) office machines, high-density server blades, and enclosed 2.5-inch SATA Solid-State Drives.

cells.value: Designed to balance power-draw, heat generation, and physical footprint. This configuration populates portable computing hosts and modern high-density blade servers, and shares an identical mounting profile with legacy enclosed flash-memory upgrades.

rows.hint: This size is found inside legacy ultra-portable sub-notebooks, early generations of thin netbooks, and classic consumer electronics like legacy portable media players (e.g., older iPods).

cells.value: 3,600 RPM / 4,200 RPM (or Solid-State)

A specialty niche configuration constrained to highly restrictive mechanical spaces. It operates at lower rotational speeds to mitigate structural thermal buildup and is typically isolated to legacy thin host equipment and vintage consumer electronics.
